topics.name: What is Data Architecture
topics.explanation: Data Architecture defines the models, policies, rules, and standards that govern how data is collected, stored, processed, and accessed across an organization. It is the blueprint that aligns data assets with business goals, ensuring data is reliable, available, secure, and scalable.
key_components: Data Sources — databases, APIs, files, streams, SaaS tools
Ingestion Layer — how data enters the system (batch, streaming, CDC)
Storage Layer — where data lives (data lake, warehouse, lakehouse)
Processing Layer — how data is transformed (Spark, Flink, dbt)
Serving Layer — how data is consumed (dashboards, APIs, ML models)
Governance Layer — policies, security, lineage, quality
goals: Single source of truth for business data
Scalable to handle growing data volumes
Reliable with minimal downtime and data loss

topics.name: Evolution of Data Architectures
topics.explanation: Data architectures have evolved in response to growing data volumes, real-time requirements, and the shift to cloud.

timeline.description: Centralized RDBMS. ETL pipelines feeding structured warehouses. IBM DB2, Oracle, Teradata.
timeline.limitations: Cannot handle unstructured data, poor scalability, expensive

timeline.description: Enterprise data warehouses with ETL tools (Informatica, SSIS). Star/snowflake schemas.
timeline.limitations: Rigid schemas, slow to change, high cost

timeline.description: HDFS + MapReduce for distributed storage/processing. Handle petabytes of unstructured data.
timeline.limitations: Complex operations, slow MapReduce, no ACID, hard to use

timeline.description: Batch layer (Hadoop) + Speed layer (Storm/Spark Streaming) + Serving layer.
timeline.limitations: Two codebases to maintain (batch and stream logic duplicated)

timeline.description: Single stream-first pipeline. Data lakes (S3/ADLS) as cheap central storage.
timeline.limitations: Data swamps, no ACID, poor query performance, schema chaos

timeline.description: Delta Lake, Iceberg, Hudi bring ACID to lakes. Cloud warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery). dbt for transformation.
Decentralized domain-owned data products. Federated governance. Real-time everywhere.
timeline.limitations: Organizational change required, not a purely technical solution

topics.name: Batch vs Streaming vs Hybrid
topics.explanation: Three fundamental processing paradigms with different latency, complexity, and cost trade-offs.

comparison_table.batch: End-of-day reports, ML training, large ETL
comparison_table.streaming: Fraud detection, live dashboards, IoT alerts

comparison_table.hybrid: Spark Structured Streaming, Delta Live Tables

batch.flow: Midnight cron → Spark reads all orders from S3 → aggregates by product/region → writes summary to Redshift → Tableau refreshes

streaming.flow: Transaction event → Kafka topic → Flink reads → applies ML model → if fraud score > 0.9 → emit alert event → block transaction

hybrid.flow: Kafka stream → Spark Structured Streaming → micro-batch every 5 min → write to Delta table → dashboard queries Delta

topics.name: CAP Theorem
topics.explanation: CAP Theorem (Brewer's Theorem) states that a distributed system can guarantee at most TWO of three properties simultaneously: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance.

properties.description: Every read receives the most recent write or an error. All nodes see the same data at the same time.
Every request receives a response (not an error), though it may not be the most recent data.
The system continues operating even if network partitions (communication failures between nodes) occur.

cap_combinations.description: Sacrifices availability. On partition, system returns error rather than stale data.
cap_combinations.use_case: Financial systems where accuracy is critical

cap_combinations.description: Sacrifices consistency. On partition, returns possibly stale data but stays available.
cap_combinations.use_case: Shopping carts, user sessions, high-availability apps

cap_combinations.description: Only possible without network partitions — not realistic in distributed systems.
cap_combinations.use_case: Single-node systems where partition tolerance is not needed

pacelc_extension.name: PACELC
pacelc_extension.description: Extends CAP: even without partitions (E), a system must trade off Latency (L) vs Consistency (C).
pacelc_extension.note: More practical than CAP for modern distributed system design

topics.de_relevance: When designing data pipelines, CAP determines: Should the pipeline prioritize delivering data immediately (AP) or only deliver when fully consistent (CP)? Kafka is AP for message delivery. PostgreSQL is CA for single-node. Cassandra is AP for high-write workloads.

topics.name: Data Flow Patterns
topics.explanation: Data flow patterns describe how data moves between systems in an architecture.

patterns.description: Direct connection between source and destination system. Simple but creates tight coupling and spaghetti integrations at scale.
patterns.drawback: N*(N-1) connections for N systems. Brittle when sources or destinations change.

patterns.description: Central hub (data warehouse or message broker) that all systems connect to. Reduces point-to-point connections.
patterns.drawback: Hub becomes a single point of failure and bottleneck.

patterns.description: Data flows through a sequence of processing stages. Output of each stage is input to the next.
patterns.diagram: Ingest → Validate → Transform → Enrich → Load → Serve
patterns.drawback: Failure in any stage blocks all downstream stages.

patterns.description: Producers publish events to a topic. Multiple consumers subscribe independently. Decoupled.
patterns.diagram: Producer → Kafka Topic → [Consumer A, Consumer B, Consumer C]
patterns.drawback: Harder to debug, eventual consistency, ordering guarantees complex.

patterns.description: Data remains in domain-owned systems. A query engine federates across multiple sources at query time.
patterns.diagram: Query Engine → [Domain DB A, Domain DB B, Domain DB C]
patterns.drawback: High query latency, complex governance across domains.

topics.name: Lambda Architecture
topics.explanation: Lambda Architecture handles massive data volumes by combining batch and streaming processing in parallel. Coined by Nathan Marz (2011). Serves as the foundation for understanding modern DE architectures.

layers.responsibility: Stores the master dataset (immutable, append-only). Periodically recomputes batch views from scratch.
Processes real-time data to fill the latency gap of the batch layer. Views are approximate and temporary.
Merges batch views and real-time views to answer queries. Responds to ad-hoc queries.

data_flow.description: All incoming data goes to BOTH batch and speed layers simultaneously
flow: 1. New event arrives (e.g., user click)
2. Event written to immutable master store (HDFS/S3)
3. Event also sent to speed layer (Kafka → Spark Streaming)
4. Speed layer computes real-time view (last few hours)
5. Batch layer recomputes full historical view (nightly)
6. Serving layer merges batch view + speed view
7. Query hits serving layer and gets merged result

example.use_case: Real-time analytics for a news website
example.batch_layer: Nightly Spark job reads all page views from S3, computes total views per article since launch
example.speed_layer: Kafka → Spark Streaming computes views in the last 1 hour

drawbacks: Two separate codebases for batch and streaming logic — must be kept in sync
High operational complexity — running two processing systems
Batch layer latency means stale data until next batch run
Reprocessing the full dataset is expensive and slow
Debugging across two systems is difficult
topics.solutions_evolution: These drawbacks led to Kappa Architecture (stream-only) and eventually Lakehouse architecture

topics.name: Kappa Architecture
topics.explanation: Kappa Architecture (Jay Kreps, 2014) simplifies Lambda by eliminating the batch layer entirely. Everything is treated as a stream. Historical reprocessing is done by replaying events from the log (Kafka retention).
topics.core_principle: A stream processing system that can replay historical data IS a batch system. No need to maintain two separate systems.

layers.responsibility: All data — historical and real-time — is processed as a stream. Kafka retains full event history. To reprocess, reset the consumer offset to the beginning.
Stores the output of stream processing for query serving.

flow: 1. Events stream into Kafka (with long retention, e.g., 1 year)
2. Flink/Kafka Streams consumes and processes events
3. Results written to serving layer
4. For reprocessing: deploy new processing job, reset offset to beginning, recompute

example.setup: Kafka topic 'user_events' retains 1 year of data
example.normal_flow: Flink reads new events → computes per-user session stats → writes to Cassandra
example.reprocessing: New feature: add geo-enrichment. Deploy new Flink job → reset offset to Jan 1 → replay all events → recompute with geo-enrichment → overwrite serving layer

drawbacks: Long reprocessing times for very large historical datasets
Kafka storage costs increase with long retention periods
Streaming logic is more complex than batch SQL (state management, ordering)
Not ideal for complex aggregations that are naturally batch-oriented
Backfilling years of data through a stream system can be slow
topics.solutions_evolution: Lakehouse architecture addresses these by providing efficient batch queries on the same data store

comparison_table.lambda: Re-run batch job on raw data
When batch and streaming logic differ significantly
comparison_table.kappa: When all processing can be modeled as streaming

lambda: Complex aggregations that are better expressed in batch SQL
Team has strong batch SQL skills (Hive/Spark SQL)
Historical analytics are a primary use case
kappa: Simplicity of a single processing model is a priority
All use cases can be framed as continuous queries

topics.name: Data Warehouse
topics.explanation: A Data Warehouse is a centralized, structured repository optimized for analytical queries (OLAP). Data is cleaned, transformed, and modeled (star/snowflake schema) before loading. Schema-on-write approach.
characteristics: Highly optimized for SQL reads and aggregations

layers.description: Raw data loaded from sources — not yet cleaned
Cleaned, conformed, integrated data (3NF or Data Vault)
Star/snowflake schema — optimized for BI queries

tables.fact_sales: sale_id, date_id, customer_id, product_id, quantity, amount

example.query: SELECT d.quarter, p.category, SUM(f.amount) AS revenue FROM fact_sales f JOIN dim_date d ON f.date_id = d.date_id JOIN dim_product p ON f.product_id = p.product_id GROUP BY d.quarter, p.category ORDER BY revenue DESC

drawbacks: Cannot store unstructured/semi-structured data (images, JSON, logs)
High cost for storing large raw datasets
Schema must be defined upfront — inflexible for exploratory analysis
ETL pipelines are time-consuming to build and maintain

topics.name: Data Lake
topics.explanation: A Data Lake is a centralized repository that stores all data in its raw, native format — structured, semi-structured, and unstructured. Schema-on-read: schema is applied only at query time.
characteristics: Stores all data types: CSV, JSON, Parquet, images, video, logs
Cheap storage (object storage: S3, GCS, ADLS)
Supports batch, streaming, ML, and ad-hoc analytics

zones.description: Exact copy of source data, never modified. Acts as system of record.

drawbacks: Easily becomes a 'data swamp' without governance
No ACID transactions — no updates/deletes on files
Poor query performance on raw files without optimization
Schema drift and inconsistent formats over time
Hard to enforce data quality and lineage
Security and access control complex at file level

topics.name: Data Lakehouse
topics.explanation: Data Lakehouse combines the low-cost open storage of Data Lakes with the reliability, ACID transactions, and performance of Data Warehouses. Achieved through open table formats (Delta Lake, Apache Iceberg, Apache Hudi) on top of object storage.
characteristics: Support for BI, ML, and streaming on same data

architecture.table_format_layer: Delta Lake / Iceberg / Hudi — adds ACID, versioning, metadata
architecture.compute_layer: Spark, Flink, Trino, Presto, DuckDB — query engines
architecture.catalog_layer: Hive Metastore, AWS Glue, Unity Catalog — metadata management

flow: 1. Raw events land in S3 as JSON (Bronze layer)
2. Spark reads JSON, cleanses, writes as Delta table (Silver layer)
3. dbt runs aggregations on Silver, writes Gold Delta tables
4. BI tools (Tableau) and ML (MLflow) query Gold Delta tables
5. Time travel: SELECT * FROM gold.sales VERSION AS OF 5

drawbacks: Added complexity of managing table format metadata
Compaction and vacuuming need regular maintenance
Not all query engines support all table formats equally
Catalog management across teams is complex

topics.name: Data Mart
topics.explanation: A Data Mart is a subset of a Data Warehouse focused on a single business domain (sales, finance, HR). Smaller, faster, and tailored to specific user groups.

types.description: Sourced from the central data warehouse. Consistent, governed.
Sourced directly from operational systems, bypassing the warehouse. Faster to build but risky — inconsistent definitions.
Combines both: primarily from warehouse but supplements with direct source feeds.
types.diagram: Data Warehouse + Operational DB → Data Mart

drawbacks: Data mart proliferation — each team builds its own, causing inconsistencies
Independent marts create conflicting metrics ('which revenue number is right?')
Maintenance overhead of multiple marts

topics.name: Operational Data Store (ODS)
topics.explanation: An ODS is a database that integrates data from multiple operational sources for current (or near-current) operational reporting. Unlike a warehouse, it stores current data with frequent updates rather than historical aggregates.
characteristics: Near-real-time data (minutes delay vs hours for warehouse)
Acts as integration hub between OLTP and OLAP
topics.data_flow: OLTP Systems → CDC/ETL → ODS → (periodic) → Data Warehouse

example.query: SELECT c.name, o.order_status, t.ticket_status FROM customer_current_state c JOIN open_orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id JOIN active_support_tickets t ON c.id = t.customer_id WHERE c.id = 12345

drawbacks: Not optimized for complex analytical queries (that's what the warehouse is for)
Data freshness still has some lag vs true real-time
Requires careful capacity planning for write-heavy workloads

topics.name: Data Warehouse vs Data Lake vs Data Lakehouse
Medallion Architecture (Bronze / Silver / Gold)
topics.explanation: Medallion Architecture organizes data into three progressive quality layers inside a Lakehouse. Each layer refines the data further. Coined by Databricks. Also known as Multi-Hop Architecture.

layers.purpose: Land raw data exactly as received from source. Immutable system of record.
characteristics: No transformation — exact copy of source
May contain duplicates, nulls, schema inconsistencies
layers.format: Parquet or Delta, original schema preserved

example.note: raw_json_payload stores the full original JSON for auditability

layers.purpose: Cleansed, deduplicated, validated, and standardized data. Enterprise-wide single source of truth.
characteristics: NULL handling and data type casting
Joins between related entities may happen here
layers.format: Delta Lake / Iceberg with enforced schema

transformations: CAST amount from string to DECIMAL
Filter out rows with NULL order_id

layers.purpose: Business-ready, aggregated, enriched data optimized for specific use cases (BI, ML, reporting).
characteristics: Named and structured for business users

example.query: SELECT sale_date, region, SUM(amount) AS total_revenue FROM silver.orders o JOIN silver.customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id GROUP BY sale_date, region

pipeline: 1. Kafka event → Bronze Delta table (append raw JSON)
2. Spark Structured Streaming → Bronze to Silver (cleanse, deduplicate, cast)
3. dbt scheduled job → Silver to Gold (aggregate, join, business logic)
4. Tableau / ML Model queries Gold layer

drawbacks: Storage cost multiplied across three layers
Pipeline latency compounded at each hop
Managing schema changes across all three layers is complex
Gold layer can proliferate with too many domain-specific tables
solutions: Use Delta VACUUM to clean up old Bronze files
Implement Delta Live Tables (DLT) for automated pipeline management
Use data contracts to govern Gold table schemas
Limit Gold tables to well-defined domains

topics.name: Data Mesh
topics.explanation: Data Mesh is a decentralized sociotechnical architecture where data ownership is distributed to domain teams rather than a central data team. Each domain treats its data as a product. Introduced by Zhamak Dehghani (2019).

four_principles.description: Each business domain (orders, payments, inventory) owns, produces, and serves its own data. No central data team owns everything.
four_principles.example: The Orders team owns the orders data product. They manage its pipeline, quality, and schema — not a central DE team.

four_principles.description: Each domain's data must be treated as a product with SLAs, documentation, discoverability, and quality guarantees — not just a by-product of operations.
A central platform team provides the infrastructure tooling (storage, compute, cataloging, lineage) that domain teams use autonomously. They don't build pipelines — they build the platform.
Governance policies (PII tagging, SLAs, retention) are defined centrally but enforced automatically via the platform — not manually by a central team.
four_principles.example: Platform auto-tags columns named 'email' or 'ssn' as PII. Masking applied automatically at query time.

example.platform_team: Provides S3, Glue catalog, Databricks workspace, DataHub, monitoring

drawbacks: Requires significant organizational change — not just a technical solution
Domain teams need data engineering skills (federated DE knowledge)
Risk of duplicated tooling and effort across domains
Federated governance is hard to enforce consistently
Discoverability across many data products requires excellent cataloging
Interoperability between domain data products needs careful standards
solutions: Strong platform team to remove toil for domain teams
Standardized data product templates and CI/CD
Automated governance via policy-as-code
Centralized data catalog with domain-level ownership
Data contracts between producer and consumer domains

topics.name: Data Fabric
topics.explanation: Data Fabric is an architecture that provides a unified, intelligent data management layer across heterogeneous, distributed environments. Unlike Data Mesh (organizational), Data Fabric is primarily a technology-driven approach using AI/ML to automate data integration, governance, and access.
key_capabilities: Active metadata — AI learns from how data is used to make recommendations
Knowledge graph — maps relationships between all data assets
Automated data integration across clouds, on-premise, and edge
Unified access layer — one endpoint to query data anywhere
Automated governance — policies applied across all data

data_mesh_vs_data_fabric.data_mesh: Organizational paradigm — decentralize ownership to domains
data_mesh_vs_data_fabric.data_fabric: Technology paradigm — unify access across distributed systems via intelligent automation
data_mesh_vs_data_fabric.key_difference: They are complementary. Data Mesh tells you WHO owns data. Data Fabric tells you HOW to connect and access it.

drawbacks: Heavy vendor dependency
High cost of enterprise fabric platforms
AI-driven recommendations require large volumes of metadata to be useful
Complex to implement across truly heterogeneous environments

topics.name: Data Hub
topics.explanation: A Data Hub is a centralized integration point that collects, harmonizes, and distributes data between systems. It acts as an operational integration layer rather than an analytical store. Manages master data and operational data flows.
topics.difference_from_warehouse: Data Warehouse = analytical queries on historical data. Data Hub = operational integration, master data management, real-time data distribution.
components: Master Data Management (MDM) — golden record for customers, products
Data Integration — connectors to source systems
Data Distribution — publish data to consuming systems
Metadata Management — catalog of what data exists
Data Quality — validation at integration point

example.hub_output: Golden customer record with merged, deduplicated attributes

drawbacks: Hub becomes a critical single point of failure
Tight coupling — all systems depend on the hub
Can become a bottleneck at high throughput

topics.name: Event-Driven Architecture
topics.explanation: Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) is a design paradigm where components communicate by producing and consuming events asynchronously. Systems are decoupled — producers don't know about consumers.

core_concepts.description: An immutable record of something that happened. Contains: event type, timestamp, payload, source.
Publishes events when something happens in the business domain.
core_concepts.example: Order service publishes 'order_placed' event when customer completes checkout

core_concepts.description: Routes, stores, and delivers events between producers and consumers.
Subscribes to events and reacts. Multiple consumers can independently process the same event.
core_concepts.example: Inventory service, notification service, analytics pipeline all consume 'order_placed' independently

flow: Customer places order → Order Service emits 'order_placed' to Kafka
Inventory Service consumes → reserves stock
Payment Service consumes → charges customer → emits 'payment_processed'
Notification Service consumes → sends confirmation email
Analytics Pipeline consumes → updates real-time sales dashboard
Fraud Service consumes → runs fraud score asynchronously
example.benefit: All 5 consumers operate independently. Adding a 6th consumer requires zero changes to the Order Service.

patterns.description: Store all state changes as events. Rebuild state by replaying events.
Command Query Responsibility Segregation — separate write model from read model.
Manage distributed transactions via a sequence of events and compensating actions.

drawbacks: Eventual consistency — consumers may lag behind producers
Debugging asynchronous flows is complex
Event schema changes break consumers if not managed carefully
Ordering guarantees across partitions are complex
Duplicate event delivery requires idempotent consumers
solutions: Schema Registry (Confluent) to manage and version event schemas
Distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry) for debugging event flows
Idempotency keys to handle duplicate processing
Dead letter queues for failed event processing

topics.name: Microservices Data Architecture
topics.explanation: In Microservices Architecture, each service owns its own database (Database-per-Service pattern). No shared databases. Services communicate via APIs or events. This creates data integration challenges for analytics.

database_per_service_pattern.description: Each microservice has its own private database. No other service can directly access it.

topics.analytics_challenge: How do you run reports across data owned by different services with different databases?

solutions_for_analytics.description: Each service emits events to Kafka. Data platform consumes all events into data lake for analytics.
solutions_for_analytics.flow: Services → Kafka → Kafka Connect → S3/Delta Lake → dbt → Warehouse → BI

solutions_for_analytics.description: Debezium captures changes from each service's DB and streams to central analytics store.
solutions_for_analytics.flow: Service DB → Debezium CDC → Kafka → Analytics Lake

solutions_for_analytics.description: Analytics service calls APIs of each microservice to pull data. Slow but simple.
solutions_for_analytics.drawback: High API load, tight coupling, slow for large datasets

drawbacks: Distributed transactions are very complex (no cross-service ACID)
Data consistency across services is eventual
Joining data across services requires event streaming or API calls
Analytics teams must understand many different data models

topics.name: Batch Ingestion
topics.explanation: Data is collected and loaded in bulk at scheduled intervals (hourly, daily, weekly). The entire dataset or a large chunk is processed at once.
characteristics: High throughput — processes large volumes efficiently
Higher latency — data is stale between runs
Good for large historical loads and reporting

example.scenario: Daily sales ingestion from PostgreSQL to S3

tasks: extract_orders: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE updated_at >= '{{ ds }}' AND updated_at < '{{ tomorrow_ds }}'
write_to_s3: write as Parquet to s3://datalake/raw/orders/date={{ ds }}/
trigger_spark_job: process raw Parquet into Silver Delta table

drawbacks: Data is stale between batch runs
Large batch runs can overload source systems
Failed batches require reprocessing the full batch
solutions: Incremental loads instead of full loads
Watermark-based extraction to minimize source load
Idempotent writes to safely rerun failed batches

topics.name: Micro-Batch Ingestion
topics.explanation: Data is processed in small batches at very short intervals (every few seconds to minutes). Bridges the gap between batch and true streaming. Used by Spark Structured Streaming's trigger interval.

example.behavior: Every 30 seconds: reads all Kafka messages since last trigger, processes, writes to Delta

drawbacks: Not truly real-time — 30-second lag may be unacceptable for some use cases
Checkpoint management adds overhead
Small file problem if trigger interval is too short
solutions: Tune trigger interval based on latency requirements
Use Delta Lake compaction to merge small files
Use continuous processing mode for sub-second latency

topics.name: Real-Time / Streaming Ingestion
topics.explanation: Events are ingested and processed individually or in very small groups as they arrive, with millisecond-to-second latency.

flow: User clicks on webpage
Browser sends event to tracking API
API publishes to Kafka topic 'clickstream' (< 10ms)
Flink consumer reads, enriches with user profile
Writes to Elasticsearch for real-time dashboard
End-to-end latency: ~100ms

drawbacks: Complex state management for aggregations over time windows
Handling late-arriving or out-of-order data
Higher infrastructure cost (always-on consumers)
Exactly-once delivery is complex to implement correctly
solutions: Watermarks to handle late data
Kafka transactions + Flink checkpointing for exactly-once
Separate streaming path for real-time and batch for historical

topics.name: Change Data Capture (CDC)
topics.explanation: CDC captures row-level changes (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) from a source database's transaction log and streams them downstream. Enables near-real-time sync without polling queries.

cdc_types.description: Reads the database transaction/WAL log (PostgreSQL WAL, MySQL binlog). Most efficient. Zero impact on source.
Polls source table for rows where updated_at > last_run_time. Simple but misses hard deletes.
cdc_types.drawback: Misses DELETE operations. Requires updated_at column.

cdc_types.description: Database triggers write changes to an audit table. Captured from audit table.
cdc_types.drawback: Adds write overhead to source system.

full_cdc_pipeline.flow: PostgreSQL WAL → Debezium → Kafka (topic: db.orders) → Kafka Connect S3 Sink → Delta Lake → dbt → Gold tables

drawbacks: Log-based CDC requires DB-level permissions (REPLICATION role)
Schema changes in source can break CDC pipeline
Initial snapshot for large tables takes significant time
Transaction log retention must be sufficient
solutions: Schema Registry to handle schema evolution in CDC events
Monitor log retention and alert before logs are purged
Use Debezium's snapshot mode for initial full load

topics.name: Full Load vs Incremental Load
topics.explanation: Two fundamental strategies for loading data from source to destination.

comparison_table.incremental: Only new or changed rows since last run
Only with soft deletes or CDC

comparison_table.full_load: Small tables, initial load, no updated_at column
comparison_table.incremental: Large tables, frequent changes, has updated_at or CDC

incremental_example.extract_sql: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE updated_at > (SELECT last_loaded_at FROM _etl_watermarks WHERE table_name = 'orders')
incremental_example.update_watermark: UPDATE _etl_watermarks SET last_loaded_at = NOW() WHERE table_name = 'orders'

topics.name: Push vs Pull Ingestion
topics.explanation: Two models for how data flows from source to destination.

comparison_table.pull: Scheduled SQL query, API polling, SFTP download

comparison_table.push: Consumer has less control over timing

comparison_table.pull: DB extracts, REST API polling, FTP files

topics.name: ETL (Extract, Transform, Load)
topics.explanation: ETL extracts data from sources, transforms it in an intermediate processing layer (outside the destination), and loads the clean, structured data into the target warehouse. Traditional approach.

flow.extract: Pull data from sources (RDBMS, APIs, files, Kafka)
flow.transform: Clean, validate, aggregate, join in a processing engine (Spark, SSIS, Informatica)
flow.load: Write transformed data into destination (warehouse, data mart)

example.extract: Airflow operator runs: SELECT * FROM postgres.orders WHERE date = '{{ ds }}'
example.load: Write final DataFrame to Redshift via COPY from S3

drawbacks: Transformation outside warehouse wastes compute — data moved twice
Schema must be defined before loading (rigid)
Long pipeline time — transform must complete before any load
Hard to handle schema evolution in transform layer

topics.name: ELT (Extract, Load, Transform)
topics.explanation: ELT loads raw data into the destination first (data lake or warehouse), then transforms it using the destination's compute power. Modern approach enabled by scalable cloud warehouses.

flow.extract: Pull raw data from sources as-is
flow.load: Load raw data directly into landing zone (S3, Snowflake raw schema, BigQuery staging)
flow.transform: Run SQL/dbt transformations inside the warehouse or on the data lake

example.extract_load: Fivetran extracts from PostgreSQL → loads raw to Snowflake RAW_DB.ORDERS (no transformation)

dbt_models: stg_orders.sql: SELECT *, CAST(amount AS DECIMAL(10,2)) FROM raw.orders
mart_daily_sales.sql: Aggregate by date and region

advantages_over_etl: Raw data preserved — can re-transform anytime
SQL-based transforms are easier for analysts
drawbacks: Raw sensitive data lands in warehouse — security concern
Warehouse compute costs can be high for complex transforms
Not ideal for very complex transformations (ML feature engineering)

topics.name: Reverse ETL
topics.explanation: Reverse ETL moves processed data FROM the warehouse BACK to operational tools (CRMs, marketing platforms, support tools). Enables data-driven operations by syncing analytics results back to the tools teams use daily.
topics.flow: Data Warehouse (Gold/Mart layer) → Reverse ETL Tool → Salesforce / HubSpot / Intercom / Zendesk

example.scenario: Syncing customer health scores to Salesforce
example.warehouse_query: SELECT customer_id, churn_probability, lifetime_value, segment FROM mart.customer_health
example.destination: Salesforce CRM — account object updated with churn score field
example.business_value: Sales reps see churn probability directly in Salesforce — can prioritize outreach

drawbacks: Risk of overwriting operational data with stale warehouse data
API rate limits of destination tools throttle sync speed
Identity resolution (matching warehouse customer_id to CRM contact_id) is complex
Bi-directional sync creates circular dependency risk

topics.name: Stream Processing
topics.explanation: Processing data continuously as it arrives, event by event or in tiny windows. State can be maintained across events for aggregations, joins, and pattern detection.

key_concepts.event_time: When the event actually occurred (in the data payload)
key_concepts.processing_time: When the event was processed by the stream engine
key_concepts.importance: Out-of-order events cause incorrect results if only processing time is used

key_concepts.description: Maintaining state across events (running counts, session tracking, join lookups)
key_concepts.example: Count user clicks per session — must remember click count as new clicks arrive

key_concepts.description: Grouping events into time buckets for aggregation

example.scenario: Real-time revenue per product category (5-minute tumbling window)

drawbacks: Complex state management and fault tolerance
Late data handling requires watermarks and allowed lateness
Hard to debug and test compared to batch
Expensive always-on infrastructure

topics.name: Batch Processing
topics.explanation: Processing a bounded, finite dataset in a single scheduled run. All data is available upfront. Simpler programming model than streaming.
drawbacks: High latency — data only available after batch completes
Resource spikes during batch windows
Failed batches must reprocess entire dataset

topics.name: Hybrid Processing
topics.explanation: Combines batch and streaming in a unified pipeline. Modern lakehouses support both on the same data store. Also called unified batch-stream processing.

example.streaming_path: Kafka → Spark Structured Streaming → append to Delta table (Silver) every 30 seconds
example.batch_path: dbt runs nightly aggregation on Silver Delta table → Gold table
example.query: BI tools query Gold Delta (batch aggregates) AND can also query Silver Delta for recent data

drawbacks: Complexity of managing both processing models
Different latency SLAs for batch vs streaming portions

topics.name: In-Place Transformation
topics.explanation: Transformations happen directly on the data in the storage layer without moving data to a separate processing engine. Common in SQL-first ELT environments.

example.flow: Raw data in Snowflake → dbt runs CREATE TABLE AS SELECT (CTAS) within Snowflake → no data leaves Snowflake
example.advantage: Zero data movement, leverage Snowflake's MPP compute, SQL-native

drawbacks: Complex ML transforms (feature engineering) hard to express in SQL
Warehouse compute costs for heavy transformations
Limited to SQL — non-SQL logic requires UDFs

topics.name: Partitioning Strategies
topics.explanation: Partitioning physically divides data into separate folders/files based on column values. Queries that filter on partition columns skip irrelevant partitions (partition pruning), dramatically improving performance.

types.best_for: Time-series data, event logs, transactional data

types.description: Distribute rows evenly via hash function.
types.best_for: Even distribution for joins, avoiding data skew

drawbacks: Over-partitioning creates too many small files (small file problem)
Wrong partition key = no pruning benefit
Partition column cardinality must be reasonable (not unique user IDs)
solutions: Partition on low-to-medium cardinality columns
Combine with Z-ordering (Databricks) for high-cardinality filter columns
Run compaction regularly to merge small partition files

topics.name: Bucketing
topics.explanation: Bucketing distributes data into a fixed number of buckets using a hash of the bucket column. Unlike partitioning, buckets are fixed-count files. Improves join performance when both tables are bucketed on the join key.

example.benefit: JOIN between orders_bucketed and customers_bucketed (both bucketed on customer_id): no shuffle needed — matching buckets are joined directly
example.speedup: Eliminates shuffle in distributed joins — major performance gain

drawbacks: Fixed bucket count — choosing wrong count causes skew or overhead
Both tables must be bucketed on same key and count for join optimization
Not supported in all query engines

topics.name: Compaction
topics.explanation: Compaction merges many small files into fewer large files. The small file problem occurs when streaming or frequent micro-batch writes create thousands of tiny files, degrading query performance.

small_file_problem.cause: Spark Structured Streaming writes one small file per micro-batch. 1000 micro-batches = 1000 files per partition.
small_file_problem.impact: Each file = one HDFS/S3 metadata call. 1M files = 1M metadata calls = very slow queries.
small_file_problem.example: Before compaction: 10,000 files × 1MB = 10GB. After: 10 files × 1GB = 10GB (same data, much faster to query)

compaction_approaches.description: Merges small files and reorders data by Z-order for better data skipping
Merges small files in Iceberg tables
Hudi compacts MOR (Merge-On-Read) delta logs into base files automatically

drawbacks: Compaction is compute-intensive — schedule during low-traffic windows
Compaction rewrites files — metadata versioning grows (need VACUUM too)

topics.name: Tiered Storage
topics.explanation: Different storage tiers offer different cost/performance trade-offs. Data moves between tiers based on access frequency and age.

tiers.use_case: Last 7 days of transactional data, real-time dashboards

tiers.description: Rarely accessed, very low cost, higher retrieval latency

lifecycle_policy_example.cost_saving: Moving 1TB from Standard to Glacier Deep Archive: ~$23/month → $1/month

topics.name: File Formats (Parquet, ORC, Avro, Delta, Iceberg)
topics.explanation: Choosing the right file format has major impact on storage cost, query performance, and compatibility.

formats.description: Most widely used analytical format. Columnar storage enables reading only needed columns. Excellent compression.
formats.best_for: Analytical queries, data lakes, Spark, Hive, Athena
formats.drawback: Not ideal for row-level updates — append-only

formats.description: Columnar format optimized for Hive workloads. Better compression than Parquet in some cases. Built-in indexes.
formats.drawback: Less ecosystem support than Parquet outside Hive

formats.description: Row-based format with rich schema (JSON-defined). Excellent for streaming (Kafka) and row-level serialization.
formats.best_for: Kafka message serialization, schema evolution, write-heavy workloads
formats.drawback: Poor analytical query performance (must read all columns)

formats.description: Parquet files + transaction log (_delta_log). Adds ACID, time travel, schema enforcement, and DML to data lakes.
formats.best_for: Databricks-centric lakehouses, ACID on data lake

formats.description: Open table format with rich metadata layer. Supports hidden partitioning, time travel, schema evolution, and row-level deletes.
formats.best_for: Multi-engine environments (Spark + Trino + Flink on same table)

topics.name: Compression Strategies
topics.explanation: Compression reduces storage size and I/O costs. The right codec balances compression ratio vs CPU overhead.

codecs.best_for: Archival, cold storage where read speed matters less
Best balance of speed and ratio — modern default

topics.recommendation: Use ZSTD for new Parquet data lake tables — best compression ratio with good read performance. Use Snappy for existing Spark pipelines (default). Use GZIP for cold archival.

topics.name: Apache Hudi
topics.explanation: Hudi (Hadoop Upserts Deletes and Incrementals) is an open table format designed for record-level upserts and incremental processing on data lakes. Originally built at Uber for their ingestion use case.

types.description: On write, new Parquet files are created rewriting the affected files. Read performance is excellent (pure Parquet). Write-heavy workloads are costly.
Writes are appended as delta log files (Avro). Periodically compacted into base Parquet. Faster writes, slightly slower reads (merge at read time).

example.upsert:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import lit

# Upsert new/changed orders into Hudi table
new_records.write.format('hudi')\
  .option('hoodie.table.name', 'orders')\
  .option('hoodie.datasource.write.recordkey.field', 'order_id')\
  .option('hoodie.datasource.write.precombine.field', 'updated_at')\
  .option('hoodie.datasource.write.operation', 'upsert')\
  .mode('append')\
  .save('s3://lake/hudi/orders')

drawbacks: More complex than plain Parquet — requires Hudi-aware reader
MOR tables require compaction scheduling
Smaller community than Iceberg for non-Spark engines
COW writes are expensive on large partitions

topics.name: Apache Iceberg
topics.explanation: Apache Iceberg is an open table format for huge analytic datasets. Designed for correctness and performance at Netflix scale. Key innovation: hidden partitioning and pointer-based metadata for atomic operations.

layers.description: Tracks the current metadata pointer for each table
metadata.json → snapshot → manifest list → manifest files
layers.note: Each commit creates a new snapshot pointing to a new manifest list
Files are never modified in-place — new files written on each commit

key_features.description: Partition transforms (year, month, bucket, truncate) are applied automatically. Query writers don't need to know partition layout.
key_features.example: CREATE TABLE orders PARTITIONED BY (days(order_date), bucket(16, customer_id)); -- No need to filter on year/month separately

SELECT * FROM catalog.db.orders FOR SYSTEM_VERSION AS OF 5;
SELECT * FROM catalog.db.orders FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF '2024-01-01 00:00:00';
DELETE FROM catalog.db.customers WHERE customer_id = 12345; -- Works on Iceberg

drawbacks: Catalog management adds operational overhead
Metadata file explosion on large tables with many small commits
Not all engines support all Iceberg features equally (v1 vs v2)
Requires compaction and metadata cleanup (expire_snapshots)

topics.name: Delta Lake
topics.explanation: Delta Lake is an open-source storage layer on top of Parquet files, developed by Databricks. It uses a transaction log (_delta_log) to enable ACID transactions, time travel, and DML on data lakes.

transaction_log.structure: JSON/Parquet checkpoint files recording every operation

key_operations.sql:
MERGE INTO delta.`s3://lake/silver/orders` AS target
USING new_orders AS source ON target.order_id = source.order_id
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET target.status = source.status
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *;

SELECT * FROM delta.`s3://lake/silver/orders` VERSION AS OF 10;
SELECT * FROM delta.`s3://lake/silver/orders` TIMESTAMP AS OF '2024-01-01';

drawbacks: Transaction log grows large on high-frequency writes — needs checkpointing
Primarily optimized for Databricks/Spark — other engines less mature
VACUUM deletes time travel history — must balance retention vs storage cost
Concurrent writes require careful coordination

topics.name: Hudi vs Iceberg vs Delta Comparison
Schema Evolution
topics.explanation: Schema evolution allows the table schema to change over time without breaking existing readers or requiring full rewrites.

supported_operations.note: Existing files return NULL for new column
Existing files still have data — metadata hides it
Iceberg and Delta track column IDs not names
BIGINT → INT risks data loss — not supported

example.scenario: Add new 'discount_amount' column to orders table
example.delta_spark:
from pyspark.sql.functions import lit
new_df = new_orders.withColumn('discount_amount', lit(0.0))
new_df.write.option('mergeSchema', 'true').format('delta').mode('append').save('s3://lake/orders')
example.iceberg_sql: ALTER TABLE catalog.db.orders ADD COLUMN discount_amount DECIMAL(10,2) COMMENT 'Discount applied to order';

topics.name: Time Travel
topics.explanation: Time travel lets you query previous versions of a table. Critical for auditing, debugging pipelines, and recovering from accidental deletes.
use_cases: Audit: what did the orders table look like before the bad data load?
Recovery: recreate Gold table from Silver snapshot before corruption
ML reproducibility: retrain model on exact dataset version used 6 months ago

examples.iceberg:
SELECT * FROM db.orders FOR SYSTEM_VERSION AS OF 8473627384;
SELECT * FROM db.orders FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF TIMESTAMP '2024-01-01 00:00:00';

drawbacks: Time travel history is limited by VACUUM / snapshot expiry settings
Keeping long history increases storage costs

topics.name: ACID on Data Lakes
topics.explanation: Traditional data lakes (raw Parquet/CSV on S3) have no ACID guarantees. Table formats (Delta/Iceberg/Hudi) add an ACID layer through optimistic concurrency control.

acid_on_lake.Atomicity: Commit writes either fully succeed (new snapshot created) or fully fail (no partial files visible)
acid_on_lake.Consistency: Schema enforcement prevents invalid data from being written
acid_on_lake.Isolation: Snapshot isolation — readers see consistent snapshot even while writes are in progress
acid_on_lake.Durability: Committed data written to durable object storage (S3/GCS); transaction log persists commit history

flow: Writer A reads current snapshot (version 5)
Writer B reads current snapshot (version 5)
Writer A commits → version 6 created
Writer B tries to commit → conflict detected (version changed)
Writer B retries with latest snapshot

topics.name: Event Streaming Architecture
topics.explanation: A distributed, persistent log of events that acts as the central nervous system of a data platform. Events are immutable facts about what happened. The log is the source of truth.

components.description: Applications, microservices, databases (via CDC), IoT devices that emit events
Persistent, ordered, distributed log of events
Stateful computation over the event stream

flink_jobs: Join orders + payments → compute revenue per minute
Detect 3 failed payments within 10 min → fraud alert

topics.name: Message Queue Architecture
topics.explanation: A message queue enables asynchronous communication between producers and consumers. Each message is consumed by exactly ONE consumer. Designed for task distribution and work queues.

difference_from_streaming.message_queue: Point-to-point. Message consumed once and deleted. No replay. (RabbitMQ, SQS)
difference_from_streaming.event_streaming: Publish-subscribe. Event persisted. Multiple consumers. Replayable. (Kafka, Kinesis)

flow: User uploads image → S3 event → SQS message added
Worker pool pulls messages from SQS
Worker processes image (resize, thumbnail)
Message deleted from queue on success
If worker fails → message reappears after visibility timeout

drawbacks: No replay — once consumed, message is gone
Single consumer per message — not suitable for fan-out
No built-in time-series analytics

topics.name: Pub/Sub Architecture
topics.explanation: Publishers send messages to topics without knowledge of subscribers. Multiple subscribers independently receive all messages from the topic. Decouples producers from consumers.

example.note: Each subscription maintains its own offset — analytics can be behind without affecting notification service

drawbacks: No ordering guarantee across messages (in most pub/sub systems)
At-least-once delivery requires idempotent consumers
Complex to debug fan-out when one subscriber is slow

topics.name: Stream-Table Duality
topics.explanation: In Kafka, a stream and a table are two views of the same data. A stream is an infinite sequence of events. A table is a snapshot of the current state (materialized view of the stream). Kafka Streams and ksqlDB implement this duality.

duality.stream_to_table: Aggregate a stream of events → current state table (running sum, latest value per key)
duality.table_to_stream: Capture changes to a table → stream of change events (CDC)

example.ksql: CREATE TABLE order_status AS SELECT order_id, LATEST_BY_OFFSET(status) AS status FROM order_events GROUP BY order_id EMIT CHANGES;

topics.name: Stateful vs Stateless Processing
topics.explanation: Stateless processors transform each event independently. Stateful processors maintain state across events (counts, sums, session data, joins).

comparison_table.stateful: Must route same key to same partition

stateful_example.use_case: Count orders per customer in last 1 hour
stateful_example.state_store: Flink stores per-customer count in RocksDB. Checkpointed to S3 every 60 seconds for fault tolerance.

topics.name: Exactly-Once Semantics
topics.explanation: Guarantees that each event is processed exactly once — no duplicates, no data loss. Hardest guarantee to achieve in distributed systems. Requires coordination between producer, broker, and consumer.

delivery_semantics.description: Fire and forget. May lose events. Never duplicates.
delivery_semantics.use_case: Metrics where occasional loss is acceptable

delivery_semantics.description: Events may be delivered multiple times. No data loss.
delivery_semantics.use_case: Most use cases with idempotent consumers

delivery_semantics.description: Each event processed exactly once. No loss, no duplicates.

kafka_exactly_once.producer_side: Enable idempotent producer: enable.idempotence=true. Each message has unique sequence number — broker deduplicates.
kafka_exactly_once.consumer_side: Kafka Streams / Flink checkpointing: offset committed atomically with output write using Kafka transactions.

drawbacks: Performance overhead (coordination, checkpointing)
Requires all components to support transactions
Not needed for all use cases — at-least-once with idempotent sinks is often sufficient

topics.name: Windowing Strategies (Tumbling, Sliding, Session)
topics.explanation: Windows group events into finite sets for aggregation in streaming pipelines.

window_types.description: Fixed-size, non-overlapping windows. Each event belongs to exactly one window.
Fixed-size, overlapping windows. Window slides by a specified interval.
Dynamic windows based on activity gaps. Window closes after inactivity gap.

example.session_1: 14:00 click, 14:10 click, 14:25 click → session closes at 14:55

window_types.description: Single window containing all events. Used with custom triggers.
window_types.use_case: Trigger processing after N events, not on time

topics.name: Watermarking & Late Data Handling
topics.explanation: Events often arrive out of order (network delays, mobile offline sync). Watermarks tell the stream processor how far behind real time it should expect events, allowing windows to close correctly.

watermark.definition: A timestamp that says: 'I guarantee no event with timestamp < watermark will arrive'
watermark.example: Watermark = max_event_time - 2_minutes. If latest event is at 14:10, watermark is at 14:08. Windows with end_time <= 14:08 can safely close.

late_data_strategies.description: Events arriving after watermark are dropped
late_data_strategies.use_case: When late events are rare and correctness is less critical

late_data_strategies.description: Window stays open for an extra duration after watermark passes, accepting late events and updating results
Route late events to a separate stream for separate handling (reprocessing queue, alerting)

topics.name: Fan-Out Pattern
topics.explanation: A single event or message is distributed to multiple downstream consumers or processing branches simultaneously. Decouples producers from multiple consumers.

example.implementation: Single Kafka topic with 5 independent consumer groups. Each group reads independently.

drawbacks: One slow consumer doesn't block others but creates consumer lag
Managing 5 independent pipelines increases operational overhead

topics.name: Fan-In Pattern
topics.explanation: Multiple input streams or sources are merged into a single output stream. Useful for aggregating events from many sources.

example.use_case: Unified audit log from multiple microservices

topics.name: Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) Pattern
topics.explanation: Events that fail processing are routed to a Dead Letter Queue instead of blocking the pipeline or being lost. Failed events can be inspected, fixed, and reprocessed.

flow: Consumer reads event from Kafka
Processing fails (malformed JSON, validation error, downstream timeout)
After N retries → event published to DLQ topic
Ops team inspects DLQ events, fixes root cause
Replay DLQ events after fix

drawbacks: DLQ can accumulate unmonitored — needs alerting
Replaying DLQ out of order can cause correctness issues

topics.name: Saga Pattern
topics.explanation: Manages long-running distributed transactions across multiple services using a sequence of local transactions. If a step fails, compensating transactions undo previous steps.

types.description: Each service publishes events and listens for events from other services. No central coordinator.
types.example: Order Service publishes order_created → Payment Service listens, charges card, publishes payment_completed → Inventory Service listens, reserves stock
types.drawback: Hard to track overall transaction state

types.description: A central Saga Orchestrator tells each service what to do and handles failures.
types.example: Orchestrator: 1. Call Payment Service → 2. Call Inventory Service → 3. Call Shipping Service. If step 2 fails → call Payment Service compensate endpoint.
types.drawback: Orchestrator becomes a single point of failure

topics.name: Outbox Pattern
topics.explanation: Guarantees that database writes and event publishing happen atomically. Solves the dual-write problem: how to write to DB AND publish to Kafka without risk of one succeeding and the other failing.
topics.dual_write_problem: Without outbox: write to DB succeeds, Kafka publish fails → DB has data, Kafka missing event → inconsistency

outbox_solution.step_1: Write to application DB table AND outbox table in same transaction
outbox_solution.step_2: Outbox relay (Debezium CDC) reads outbox table, publishes to Kafka
outbox_solution.step_3: Delete/mark outbox row after successful publish
outbox_solution.guarantee: Both DB write and Kafka publish are atomic — no dual-write inconsistency

topics.outbox_table:
CREATE TABLE outbox (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY,
  event_type VARCHAR,
  payload JSONB,
  created_at TIMESTAMP,
  published_at TIMESTAMP
);

topics.name: Circuit Breaker Pattern
topics.explanation: Prevents a pipeline from repeatedly calling a failing downstream service, giving it time to recover. Like an electrical circuit breaker — trips when too many failures occur.

states.description: Calls pass through. Failure count tracked.
Calls blocked immediately. Fallback used. Wait for reset timeout.
Allow limited test calls. If successful → close. If fail → reopen.

example.config: Trip after 5 failures in 30s. Reset after 60s.
example.behavior: 5 API timeouts in 30s → circuit opens → pipeline uses cached fallback response for 60s → half-open: 1 test call → success → circuit closes

topics.name: Retry & Backoff Pattern
topics.explanation: Automatically retry failed operations with increasing delays to avoid hammering a recovering service.

backoff_strategies.description: Double the wait time after each failure
Add random jitter to avoid thundering herd (all retries hitting service at same time)

topics.name: Idempotency Pattern
topics.explanation: An operation is idempotent if applying it multiple times produces the same result as applying it once. Critical for safe retries in distributed pipelines.

examples.non_idempotent: INSERT INTO orders VALUES (...) — duplicate rows on retry
examples.idempotent: INSERT INTO orders VALUES (...) ON CONFLICT (order_id) DO UPDATE SET ... — safe to retry

examples.non_idempotent: Append to file — duplicates on retry
examples.idempotent: Write to deterministic path (date-keyed) — overwrite on retry

topics.name: Backpressure Pattern
topics.explanation: Mechanism to slow down producers when consumers cannot keep up. Prevents consumer out-of-memory and data loss by signaling upstream to reduce throughput.

kafka_consumer_lag.scenario: Kafka consumer processing 1000 events/sec, producer sending 5000 events/sec
kafka_consumer_lag.without_backpressure: Consumer falls behind → memory overflow → OOM error
kafka_consumer_lag.with_backpressure: Consumer signals Kafka: only poll 100 records per call. Lag grows but consumer stays healthy. Auto-scale consumers to catch up.

example.flink_backpressure: Flink has built-in backpressure: if downstream operator is slow, upstream operator reduces rate automatically via credit-based flow control

topics.name: DAG-Based Orchestration
topics.explanation: Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) — tasks represented as nodes, dependencies as directed edges. The orchestrator executes tasks in topological order, respecting dependencies.

example.parallel_execution: extract_orders and extract_customers run in parallel since neither depends on the other

drawbacks: Static DAGs require code deployment for every change
Cron-based scheduling is time-based, not data-arrival-based
Airflow scheduler can become a bottleneck at scale

topics.name: Event-Driven Orchestration
topics.explanation: Pipeline tasks are triggered by events (data arrival, upstream completion, external signals) rather than time-based schedules. More reactive and efficient.

flow: Vendor uploads file to S3
S3 event notification → Lambda → triggers Airflow DAG via REST API
Airflow DAG starts processing immediately
No waiting for next cron run

drawbacks: Harder to reason about than time-based schedules
Event loss can silently skip pipeline runs
Debugging event-triggered flows requires event tracing

topics.name: Choreography vs Orchestration
topics.explanation: Two models for coordinating distributed processes.

comparison_table.choreography: Each service reacts to events independently
Hard — flow is implicit in event chain

comparison_table.orchestration: Complex multi-step pipelines with clear owner
comparison_table.choreography: Simple event chains, highly decoupled teams

topics.name: Dynamic DAGs
topics.explanation: DAGs generated programmatically at runtime based on config, database state, or external parameters. Enables parameterized and data-driven pipeline generation.

example.use_case: Generate one DAG per active client
example.airflow_code:
import yaml
clients = yaml.safe_load(open('clients.yaml'))
for client in clients:
    dag_id = f'ingest_{client["name"]}'
    dag = DAG(dag_id, schedule_interval=client['schedule'])
    with dag:
        extract = PythonOperator(task_id='extract', python_callable=extract_fn, op_kwargs={'client': client})
        load    = PythonOperator(task_id='load',    python_callable=load_fn,    op_kwargs={'client': client})
        extract >> load
    globals()[dag_id] = dag

drawbacks: Dynamic DAGs make the Airflow UI noisy with many DAGs
Hard to debug when generated DAG has issues
Airflow parses all DAG files every heartbeat — too many dynamic DAGs can slow scheduler

topics.name: SLA & Alerting Patterns
topics.explanation: SLAs define expected completion times for pipelines. Alerting notifies teams when SLAs are breached or tasks fail.

sla_types.example: Sales dashboard must show data no older than 2 hours
NULL rate for order_id must be < 0.01%

airflow_sla_example.alert_function: def sla_alert_fn(dag, task_list, blocking_task_list, slas, blocking_tis): send_slack_alert(f'SLA missed: {task_list}')

topics.name: Data Catalog Architecture
topics.explanation: A data catalog is a metadata inventory of all data assets — tables, columns, pipelines, dashboards. It enables data discovery, lineage tracking, and governance.

components.description: Schema, data types, row counts, partition info, last updated
Business definitions, owners, SLAs, data domains
Pipeline run history, data quality scores, freshness
Column-level descriptions, popularity scores, user ratings

example.search_example: Search 'customer revenue' → finds gold.customer_revenue_daily table, owner: analytics team, updated: 2 hours ago, upstream: silver.orders + silver.customers, downstream: Tableau Sales Dashboard

drawbacks: Catalog becomes outdated if not auto-populated via pipeline integration
Manual documentation efforts don't scale
Requires organizational buy-in to maintain descriptions and ownership

topics.name: Data Lineage
topics.explanation: Data lineage tracks the origin, movement, and transformation of data — where it came from, what changed it, and where it went. Essential for impact analysis and debugging.

types.description: Tracks which source columns contribute to each target column
Tracks which tables feed into which tables
Tracks which pipeline/job produced which dataset
types.example: Airflow DAG: daily_etl → Spark job: clean_orders → Delta table: silver.orders

use_cases: Impact Analysis: if silver.orders schema changes, which Gold tables and dashboards are affected?
Root Cause: incorrect revenue number → trace back to source transformation bug
GDPR: which tables contain customer_id 12345's data? (for right-to-be-forgotten)
drawbacks: Automated lineage extraction requires deep integration with all tools
Column-level lineage from complex SQL is hard to parse automatically
Lineage graphs become hard to navigate for large data platforms

topics.name: Data Observability
topics.explanation: Data observability monitors the health of data across five pillars. Like application monitoring (Datadog) but for data pipelines and datasets.

five_pillars.description: Is data being updated on schedule?
five_pillars.alert: orders table not updated in 3 hours (SLA: 1 hour)

five_pillars.description: Is the expected amount of data arriving?

five_pillars.alert: order_amount mean dropped from $85 to $2 — possible data error
bronze.raw_orders (upstream) failed — silver.orders may be stale

drawbacks: False positives from legitimate business events (holiday spikes, promotions)
Statistical anomaly detection requires tuning baseline windows
Tool cost scales with data volume

topics.name: Data Quality Framework
topics.explanation: A systematic approach to measuring, monitoring, and enforcing data quality across pipelines.

quality_dimensions.check: Same entity has same values across tables
quality_dimensions.example: customer_id in orders exists in customers table

quality_dimensions.check: No duplicate records on key columns

great_expectations_example.code:
from great_expectations.dataset import PandasDataset
ds = PandasDataset(orders_df)
ds.expect_column_values_to_not_be_null('order_id')
ds.expect_column_values_to_be_in_set('status', ['pending','shipped','delivered'])
ds.expect_column_values_to_be_between('amount', 0.01, 100000)
results = ds.validate()

topics.name: Data Contracts
topics.explanation: A data contract is a formal, machine-readable agreement between a data producer and consumer defining the schema, semantics, SLAs, and quality guarantees of a dataset.
contract_components: Schema: column names, data types, nullable constraints
Semantics: what each column means (business definitions)
Versioning: how breaking changes are communicated and handled
Owner: who is responsible for this data product

topics.name: Schema Registry
topics.explanation: A centralized repository that stores and manages schema versions for event data (Avro, Protobuf, JSON Schema). Ensures producers and consumers use compatible schemas.

flow.producer:
1. Producer registers schema with registry → gets schema_id
2. Producer serializes event: [magic_byte][schema_id][avro_payload]
3. Sends to Kafka
flow.consumer:
1. Consumer reads [schema_id] from message
2. Fetches schema from registry
3. Deserializes payload using schema

compatibility_modes.description: New schema can read data written by old schema (add optional fields)
Old schema can read data written by new schema

drawbacks: Schema Registry becomes critical infrastructure — must be highly available
Breaking schema changes require all consumers to update
Complex to manage across many teams and schemas

topics.name: PII & Data Masking Architecture
topics.explanation: PII (Personally Identifiable Information) must be detected, tagged, and masked/encrypted to comply with GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA. Architecture must enforce PII handling across all layers.

pii_detection.automated: Scan column names and values for PII patterns: email, SSN, phone, name, address, IP

masking_techniques.use_case: Store encrypted, decrypt for authorized users

architecture_layers.ingestion: Detect and tag PII columns via automated scanner
architecture_layers.storage: Store PII in encrypted columns; tag in data catalog
architecture_layers.processing: Apply column masking at query time for non-privileged users
architecture_layers.serving: Row-level and column-level security in warehouse

topics.name: Separation of Storage and Compute
topics.explanation: Modern cloud data platforms decouple storage (S3/GCS/ADLS) from compute (Spark clusters, Snowflake warehouses). Scale each independently. Pay only for compute when querying.
benefits: Scale compute up for heavy workloads, scale down to zero when idle
Multiple compute engines can query the same data (Spark + Presto + Flink on same S3 data)
Storage is cheap and infinitely scalable (S3)
No data movement between storage and compute tiers

example.compute: Databricks clusters auto-scale: 0 workers (idle) → 20 workers (peak) → 0 workers
example.cost_saving: Pay for compute only during job execution. No always-on cluster.

drawbacks: Network latency between storage and compute (vs local disk)
Cloud egress charges for cross-region data access
Multiple engines on same data require careful concurrency management

topics.name: Serverless Data Architecture
topics.explanation: No servers to provision or manage. Cloud provider auto-scales compute based on demand. Pay per query/execution.

examples.description: Serverless SQL on S3. Pay per TB scanned.
Serverless warehouse. Pay per TB processed.

example.use_case: Ad-hoc query on S3 data lake with Athena
example.setup: No cluster to start. Define table in Glue Catalog pointing to S3 Parquet.
example.query: SELECT region, SUM(amount) FROM s3_orders WHERE year=2024 GROUP BY region;

drawbacks: Cold start latency for infrequent workloads
Cost unpredictable for large scan queries
Less control over performance tuning (no cluster config)
Not suitable for long-running complex ETL jobs

topics.name: Multi-Cloud Architecture
topics.explanation: Data platform spans multiple cloud providers (AWS + GCP + Azure). Avoids vendor lock-in. Uses best-of-breed services across clouds.
use_cases: Business acquired a company on different cloud
Regulatory requirement to store data in specific regions (different cloud per region)
Best service selection: GCP Vertex AI + AWS S3 + Azure AD
challenges: Data gravity — moving data between clouds incurs egress costs
Security and networking complexity across clouds
solutions: Open table formats (Iceberg) enable any engine on any cloud to query same data
Snowflake / Databricks as cross-cloud abstraction layer
Keep most data in primary cloud; replicate only necessary subsets

topics.name: Shared Nothing Architecture
topics.explanation: Each processing node has its own CPU, memory, and storage. No shared resources between nodes. Enables massive parallel processing (MPP). Used by Redshift, Snowflake, Teradata.

how_it_works.leader_node: Receives queries, generates execution plan, distributes to compute nodes
how_it_works.compute_nodes: Each node processes its own data slice in parallel
how_it_works.distribution: Data distributed across nodes by distribution key (hash or round-robin)
how_it_works.result_aggregation: Leader node collects results from all compute nodes and returns final result

example.query: SELECT region, SUM(amount) FROM orders GROUP BY region
example.execution: Each compute node sums its own orders partition → leader aggregates 8 partial sums → returns result

drawbacks: Data skew: if distribution key is poorly chosen, some nodes get much more data
Cross-node data movement (broadcasting/shuffling) is expensive
Fixed cluster size — scaling requires redistributing data

comparison_table.vertical: Bigger RDS instance, more RAM for PostgreSQL

topics.name: Sharding
topics.explanation: Sharding horizontally partitions a database across multiple nodes. Each shard holds a subset of data. Enables linear write and read scaling.

sharding_strategies.description: Shard by value range. Shard 1: A-F, Shard 2: G-M, Shard 3: N-Z
sharding_strategies.drawback: Hotspots if data not evenly distributed

drawbacks: Cross-shard joins are expensive or impossible
Re-sharding (adding nodes) requires data migration
Application must know which shard to query

topics.name: Caching Patterns
topics.explanation: Caching stores frequently accessed data in fast memory to reduce compute and database load.

patterns.flow: 1. Check cache → 2. If miss: query DB → 3. Store in cache → 4. Return
patterns.use_case: General-purpose caching. Cache only what's requested.
patterns.drawback: Cache miss = full DB query latency. Cache can have stale data.

patterns.flow: Write to cache AND DB simultaneously
patterns.use_case: Ensure cache and DB always in sync

patterns.flow: Write to cache → acknowledge → async write to DB
patterns.use_case: High-write workloads where DB can lag
patterns.drawback: Risk of data loss if cache fails before DB write

example.code:
import redis
r = redis.Redis()

# Cache-aside
def get_daily_sales(date):
    cached = r.get(f'sales:{date}')
    if cached:
        return json.loads(cached)
    # Cache miss
    result = db.query(f'SELECT SUM(amount) FROM orders WHERE date = {date}')
    r.setex(f'sales:{date}', 3600, json.dumps(result))  # TTL: 1 hour
    return result

topics.name: Predicate Pushdown
topics.explanation: Push filter conditions (WHERE clauses) to the data source layer — read only the relevant data instead of reading all data and filtering after. Dramatically reduces I/O.

example.without_pushdown: Spark reads ALL 100GB Parquet files from S3 → filters WHERE region = 'East' in memory → 95GB wasted I/O
example.with_pushdown: Spark pushes WHERE region = 'East' to Parquet reader → reads only column statistics and row groups where region = 'East' → only reads 5GB

how_parquet_enables_it: Row group statistics: min/max per column per row group
Column chunk statistics: null counts, distinct counts
If WHERE amount > 1000 and row group max_amount = 500, skip entire row group
topics.z_ordering: Delta Lake ZORDER BY (customer_id) co-locates data with same customer_id → predicate on customer_id skips most files

topics.name: Vectorized Execution
topics.explanation: Process data in batches of vectors (arrays of column values) rather than row by row. Leverages CPU SIMD (Single Instruction Multiple Data) instructions for 10-100x speedup.
topics.traditional_row_by_row: For each row: load customer_id, amount; check amount > 100; add to sum
topics.vectorized: Load 1024 amounts as array → SIMD: compare all 1024 > 100 in one instruction → sum the vector
topics.apache_arrow: Apache Arrow defines an in-memory columnar format that enables zero-copy data sharing between vectorized engines (Spark, Pandas, Flink, Parquet reader)

topics.name: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
topics.explanation: Access to data is controlled by roles. Users are assigned roles. Roles have permissions. Easier to manage than per-user permissions.

permissions: SELECT, INSERT on bronze.*, silver.*, gold.*

topics.name: Column-Level & Row-Level Security
topics.explanation: Fine-grained access control at the column or row level within a table.

column_level.description: Certain columns (PII, salary) are masked or hidden for specific roles

row_level.description: Users only see rows relevant to their region, department, or tenant

mechanisms: Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) in SQL Server/Oracle
Column-level encryption for specific sensitive columns
at_rest.example: S3 bucket policy: enforce SSE-KMS encryption on all objects. All Parquet files encrypted with KMS-managed key.

in_transit.description: Data encrypted while moving between systems
mechanisms: TLS 1.2+ for all API and database connections
VPC private endpoints to avoid public internet

topics.name: Network Security (VPC, Private Endpoints)
topics.explanation: Isolate data infrastructure in private networks. Prevent public internet exposure.

vpc_architecture.public_subnet: Load balancers, NAT gateways — exposed to internet
vpc_architecture.private_subnet: RDS, Kafka, Spark clusters — no direct internet access
vpc_architecture.data_subnet: S3, data lakes — accessed via VPC endpoints only

vpc_endpoints.description: S3 Gateway Endpoint: Spark clusters in VPC connect to S3 without internet. No egress cost. Traffic stays on AWS network.
vpc_endpoints.example:
Without VPC endpoint: Spark → Internet → S3 (egress cost + latency)
With VPC endpoint:    Spark → AWS internal network → S3 (no cost + lower latency)

topics.security_groups: Inbound: only allow Spark cluster IPs to connect to Kafka (port 9092). Block all other IPs.

topics.name: Modern Data Stack Architecture
topics.explanation: The Modern Data Stack (MDS) is a cloud-native, modular data architecture using best-of-breed SaaS tools for each layer.

example_stack.ingestion: Fivetran syncs Salesforce, Stripe, PostgreSQL → Snowflake
example_stack.bi: Looker dashboards for product and finance teams
example_stack.reverse_etl: Census syncs churn scores from Snowflake back to Salesforce

drawbacks: Vendor dependency and SaaS costs add up quickly
Data always flows through third-party tools — security concern
Less control over performance tuning vs self-managed stack

topics.name: Data Platform Architecture on AWS

ingestion.batch: AWS Glue crawlers / DMS for database migration
ingestion.files: S3 event notifications → Lambda → trigger Glue

processing.sql: Athena (serverless SQL on S3) / Redshift Spectrum

layers.governance: AWS Glue Data Catalog / Lake Formation / Macie (PII)

topics.reference_flow: Source DBs → DMS/Glue CDC → Kinesis → Lambda → S3 Bronze (Delta) → EMR Spark → S3 Silver (Delta) → Glue dbt → S3 Gold (Delta) → Redshift Spectrum → QuickSight

layers.ingestion: Auto Loader (stream from S3/ADLS) / Kafka connector
layers.bronze: Delta Lake raw tables — append-only, partitioned by date
layers.silver: Delta Live Tables (DLT) pipelines — cleanse, deduplicate, merge
layers.gold: dbt models on Databricks SQL or DLT expectations
layers.ml: MLflow tracking + Feature Store on Gold Delta tables
layers.serving: Databricks SQL Warehouse (for BI) + Model Serving (for ML)

layers.producers: Mobile app / Web app / IoT devices / Microservices
layers.processing: Apache Flink (stateful stream processing, windowed aggregations)

storage.real_time: Apache Druid or ClickHouse (sub-second OLAP queries)
storage.historical: Delta Lake / S3 (for long-term batch analytics)

serving.alerts: Flink → Kafka alerts topic → PagerDuty / Slack

example.latency: < 1 second from click to dashboard update

decision_framework.reason: Wrong balance is worse than temporary unavailability
Showing slightly stale data is better than error

decision_framework.choose: CP for writes, AP for reads
decision_framework.reason: Must be accurate for writes, ok to show slightly stale stock count

topics.name: Latency vs Throughput Trade-offs
topics.explanation: Optimizing for low latency (fast individual response) often conflicts with optimizing for high throughput (many requests per second).

examples.throughput: Smaller batch = more overhead = lower throughput
Compression = less I/O = more throughput on large datasets

decision_tree.question: Team size and skills?
decision_tree.if_large_engineering: Custom lakehouse on Databricks or open-source

decision_tree.question: Domain ownership model?
decision_tree.if_centralized: Medallion architecture with central DE team
decision_tree.if_federated: Data Mesh with domain-owned data products

decision_tree.question: Budget?
decision_tree.if_limited: Open-source stack (Spark + Iceberg + Airflow on EC2)

anti_patterns.name: Data Swamp
anti_patterns.description: Data lake with no governance, no catalog, no lineage — data exists but nobody knows what it means or trusts it
anti_patterns.solution: Implement data catalog, lineage, data contracts, and data quality checks from day 1

anti_patterns.name: Too Many Point-to-Point Pipelines
anti_patterns.description: Every team builds direct pipelines to every other team's data. N*(N-1) pipelines = spaghetti
anti_patterns.solution: Central event bus (Kafka) or data platform layer. All producers publish to platform; consumers read from platform

anti_patterns.name: Over-Engineering for Day 1
anti_patterns.description: Building Data Mesh microservices for a 5-person startup with 1GB data
anti_patterns.solution: Start simple (Postgres + dbt + Metabase). Scale architecture as data volume and team grow

anti_patterns.name: Missing Idempotency
anti_patterns.description: Non-idempotent pipelines create duplicate data on reruns after failures
anti_patterns.solution: UPSERT patterns, deterministic write paths, watermark-based incremental loads

anti_patterns.name: No Schema Management
anti_patterns.description: Source system changes column name → all downstream pipelines silently break or produce wrong results
anti_patterns.solution: Schema Registry, data contracts, schema evolution testing in CI/CD

anti_patterns.name: Ignoring Data Skew
anti_patterns.description: Hash partition on a skewed key → 90% data in 1 partition → 1 slow task holds up entire job
anti_patterns.solution: Salt keys, repartition, use salted joins, choose high-cardinality partition keys

questions.question: What is the difference between Lambda and Kappa architecture?
questions.answer: Lambda uses two separate processing pipelines: a batch layer for accuracy on historical data and a speed layer for real-time results. Kappa eliminates the batch layer — everything is a stream, with historical reprocessing done by replaying the event log from the beginning. Lambda's main drawback is maintaining two codebases with the same logic. Kappa solves this with a single streaming codebase but struggles with very large historical reprocessing.

drawbacks_and_solutions.lambda_drawback: Duplicate logic in batch and stream code must be kept in sync
drawbacks_and_solutions.kappa_drawback: Replaying years of data through streaming can be very slow
drawbacks_and_solutions.modern_solution: Data Lakehouse (Delta Lake) + unified batch-stream engines (Flink, Spark Structured Streaming) effectively supersede both

questions.question: What is a Data Lakehouse and how does it differ from a Data Lake?
questions.answer: A Data Lake stores raw data (any format) on object storage but lacks ACID guarantees, schema enforcement, and efficient querying. A Data Lakehouse adds a table format layer (Delta Lake, Iceberg, Hudi) on top of the data lake, providing ACID transactions, time travel, schema evolution, and efficient query performance — while retaining the cost and flexibility benefits of the lake.

drawbacks_and_solutions.drawback: Added complexity of table format metadata, compaction, and vacuum management
drawbacks_and_solutions.solution: Use managed platforms (Databricks) that automate compaction, vacuuming, and Delta Live Tables

questions.question: Explain the Medallion Architecture (Bronze/Silver/Gold).
questions.answer: Medallion Architecture has three progressive data quality layers. Bronze: raw, immutable source data — exact copy, no transformation. Silver: cleansed, deduplicated, standardized — single source of truth for row-level data. Gold: business-ready aggregates, domain-specific, optimized for analytics and ML. Each layer builds on the previous, improving data quality.

drawbacks_and_solutions.drawback: Storage cost multiplied 3x, pipeline latency compounded at each hop
